coredata.dc:title: Rapid detection of the biomarker for cystinuria by a metal-organic framework fluorescent sensor
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Liping
Guo, Hao
Wu, Ning
Liu, Bingqing
Wang, Mingyue
Tian, Jiaying
Ren, Borong
Yu, Zhiguo
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: Arginine (Arg) is considered a valuable biomarker for various diseases, including cystinuria, and its concentration level holds significant implications for human health. To achieve the purposes of food evaluation and clinical diagnosis, it is imperative to develop a rapid and facile method for selective and sensitive determination of Arg. In this work, a novel fluorescent material (Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66) was synthesized by encapsulating carbon dots (CDs), Eu3+ and Ag + into UiO-66. This material can serve as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting Arg. It exhibits a high sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.74 μM and a relatively broad linear range from 0–300 μM. After dispersing the composite Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 in an Arg solution, the red emission of Eu3+ center at 613 nm was significantly enhanced, while the characteristic peak of CDs center at 440 nm remained unchanged. Therefore, a ratio fluorescence probe could be constructed based on the peak height ratio of the two emission peaks to achieve selective detection of Arg. In addition, the remarkable ratiometric luminescence response induced by Arg results in a significant color transition from blue to red under UV-lamp for Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66, which was convenient for visual analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Metal-organic framework
Biomarkers
Cystinuria
Arginine

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665024), Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (20JR10RA083), Project of Gansu Excellent Doctoral (22JR5RA140), Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23), the Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE , Key Lab of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province for their financial supports.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of UiO-66 (a, b), CDs@UiO-66(c, d), and Eu3+/Ag+CDs@UiO-66 (e, f) at different magnification and (g) EDS mapping of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66.

(a) XRD patterns of the simulation UiO-66 (black line), the synthesized UiO-66 (purple line), CDs@UiO-66 (red line), Eu/CDs@UiO-66 (blue line) and Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 (green line). (b) XPS survey spectra, (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and (d) the pore size distributions of the UiO-66 and Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66.

(a) Emission spectra of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 introduced into solutions with different amino acid and metal ions. (b) The luminescence spectra of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 suspension before and after adding Arg. (d) The CIE chromaticity diagram of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 before and after adding Arg. (d) Anti-interference ability of the Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 sensor for Arg.
(a) Emission spectra of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 dispersed in different concentrations of Arg aqueous solution. (b) Linear calibration plot of I613/I440 versus the concentration of Arg in 0–300 μM.
Schematic illustration of preparation of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 and fluorescent sensing to Arg.
Comparison of analytical performances of the proposed fluorescent sensor to arginine with reported similar sensors.
Determination results of Arg in real samples by standard addition method.

head.ce:title: Rapid detection of the biomarker for cystinuria by a metal-organic framework fluorescent sensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE; Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province; College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE
Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE; Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province; College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Arginine (Arg) is considered a valuable biomarker for various diseases, including cystinuria, and its concentration level holds significant implications for human health. To achieve the purposes of food evaluation and clinical diagnosis, it is imperative to develop a rapid and facile method for selective and sensitive determination of Arg. In this work, a novel fluorescent material (Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66) was synthesized by encapsulating carbon dots (CDs), Eu3+ and Ag + into UiO-66. This material can serve as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for detecting Arg. It exhibits a high sensitivity with a detection limit of 0.74 μM and a relatively broad linear range from 0–300 μM. After dispersing the composite Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 in an Arg solution, the red emission of Eu3+ center at 613 nm was significantly enhanced, while the characteristic peak of CDs center at 440 nm remained unchanged. Therefore, a ratio fluorescence probe could be constructed based on the peak height ratio of the two emission peaks to achieve selective detection of Arg. In addition, the remarkable ratiometric luminescence response induced by Arg results in a significant color transition from blue to red under UV-lamp for Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66, which was convenient for visual analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cystinuria, also known as Sulfite Oxidase Deficiency, is an autosomal recessive disorder characterized by reduced reabsorption of cysteine and arginine in the renal tubules, resulting in increased urinary excretion of cysteine. Since cysteine is rarely soluble in acidic urine, it precipitates and hardens into crystals or stones when its concentration exceeds the saturation point [1,2]. In severe cases, obstructive nephropathy and hydronephrosis may lead to renal failure [3]. Arginine is considered a reliable biomarker for detecting cysteinuria [4,5]. Monitoring patient urine arginine level is crucial for achieving early prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of cystinuria. At present, various methods have been proposed for the detection of Arg, such as high-performance liquid chromatography [6,7], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry [8], molecular recognition technology [9] and capillary electrophoresis [10]. Although these methods are highly sensitive, they require expensive equipment, laborious pre-treatment and time-consuming operation, which limits their practical applications [11–13]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a new strategy that is easy to use, highly selective and sensitive for the detection of Arg.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials composed of metal ions or clusters interconnected by organic ligands. Due to their large surface area and ease of modification, they have garnered significant attention from researchers in various fields [14,15], and have been widely used in agriculture [16], gas storage and separation [17], light collection [18], catalysis [19] and sensing [20]. Some rare earth organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs), a branch of MOFs, are widely used in the sensing field due to excellent luminescent properties originating from the f-f electronic transitions in the inner shell [21,22]. However, although researchers are full of imagination about the structure design of Ln-MOFs, the direct synthesis of Ln-MOFs is a challenging work because lots of unfilled f orbitals make them have larger coordination number and more flexible coordination configuration [23,24]. The modification of scaffolds after synthesis into broadly unpredictable products provides appropriate insights and is an alternative strategy for preparing Ln-MOFs, which inspires and guides us to construct host guest recognition rare earth MOFs [25–27]. In addition, most reported Ln-MOFs fluorescence sensors rely on a single emission center generated by lanthanide ion or ligand, which may cause errors due to changes in external environment, thereby affecting the accuracy of detection [28–30]. In order to overcome the main limitations mentioned above, further development of ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on Ln-MOFs with two or more emission centers at a single excitation wavelength is imperative [31–35]. Therefore, constructing a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Ln-MOF through a post synthesis modification strategy is a very interesting topic [36–40].

Carbon dots (CDs) is a good fluorescent material possessing unique optical properties and superior biocompatibility [41]. In recent years, they have garnered increasing attention for their exceptional optical stability, ease of preparation, low cost, low toxicity, high chemical inertness, superior fluorescence quantum yield, and excellent water solubility [42]. According to the literature, photoluminescence is one of the most renowned optical properties exhibited by CDs. Its intensity can be easily influenced by carbon precursors and synthesis methods. The luminescent behavior of CDs can be theoretically controlled by their particle size and surface state, but it also is greatly influenced by external factors. Additionally, their tendency to agglomerate weakens their fluorescence signal [43–45]. To overcome these deficiencies, a viable strategy involves the encapsulation of CDs within MOFs, which will thereby create some functional composites with adjustable optical properties. The porosity of MOFs enables the dispersion of CDs and mitigates agglomeration. Besides, MOFs have specific adsorption and accumulation capabilities for target analytes, which can also improve the sensitivity of detection [46].

Inspired by above investigations, in this paper, a new dual-emission fluorescent material (Ag/Eu-CDs@UiO-66) was developed by packaging CDs, Eu3+ ions and Ag + ions into the parent framework of UiO-66 using a three-step post-synthetic modification strategy, and used as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for the detection of Arg (Scheme 1 ). The material possesses both the significant optical properties of CDs and Eu3+ ions and the distinct structural characteristics of UiO-66. At the same excitation wavelength (at 290 nm), it shows two different characteristic emissions. Interestingly, when Arg was added to Ag/Eu-CDs@UiO-66 suspension, the characteristic emission of CD at 440 nm remained basically unchanged, while that of Eu3+ at 613 nm was significantly enhanced. Therefore, a ratiometric probe with 440 nm as a reference wavelength was constructed for the specific detection of arginine. It is very useful for the clinical diagnosis and rapid screening of cystinuria and other diseases.
CDs were prepared from Pinellia ternate by a simple hydrothermal method in supplementary information.

UiO-66 was synthesized using a previously reported literature process with a small modification [47]. The synthesis process of CDs@UiO-66 is the same as that of UiO-66, but deionized water was replaced with a CDs solution. The detailed prepared process was provided in the supplementary information.

The preparation of Eu/CDs@UiO-66 was carried out through a simple stirring method referring to the literature [48]. Briefly, 200 mg of CDs@UiO-66 was put into a round bottom flask equipped with a magnetic stirrer containing 20 mL of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (1 mmol, 0.892 g) aqueous solution and then the mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 24 h. The resulting Eu/CD@UiO-66 were washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times to guarantee that all excess Eu(NO3)3·6H2O was removed. Subsequently, the product was dried under a vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 was prepared by referring to the previously reported method with slight modifications [49]. It was obtained by heating the mixture of 0.1 g Eu/CDs@UiO-66 and 0.170 g (1 mM) AgNO3 in 10 mL distilled water at 60 °C for 24 h. The resulting Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 was washed with distilled water and ethanol for several times to remove excess AgNO3. Lastly, the product was dried under a vacuum at 60 °C overnight.

1 mg of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 sample was dispersed in 5 mL (10−2 M) of Arg aqueous solution, and the luminescence spectra of the suspension were recorded after ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. To further evaluate the selectivity of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 for Arg, 1 mg of sample was dispersed into 5 mL of different amino acid solutions (10−2 M) including tryptophan (Try), phenylalanine (Phe), alanine (Ala), aspartic acid (Asp), cysteine (Cys), glutamic acid (Glu), glycine (Gly), histidine (His), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), threonine (Thr), valine (Val), methionine (Met), serine (Ser), proline (Pro), serine (Ser) and homocystine (Hom). After incubation for 10 min at room temperature the fluorescence spectra of the solutions were measured and compared with that in the presence of Arg.

Serum and urine samples were collected from healthy volunteers and processed before analysis. Firstly, the serum samples were treated to remove the protein. 1.5 mL acetonitrile was added into 1 mL serum sample and sonicated for 5 min. After centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 30 min, the analytical solution of serum was obtained [29]. Urine samples were centrifugally separated at 8000 rpm for 20 min and the obtained supernatant was diluted 100 times for determination. Tea samples were purchased from local markets. 2 g of tea sample was put into a round-bottomed flask containing 100 mL distilled water and refluxed at 90 °C for 30 min. The supernatant of the mixture was passed through a filter with a 0.22 μm membrane to obtain an aqueous solution containing amino acids for further analysis and application [50]. Different concentrations of Arg standard solutions were added to the above three actual samples and incubated for 10 min. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at 290 nm excitation.

Firstly, CDs were encapsulated in the UiO-66 framework structure, and then Eu3+ ions and Ag + ions were introduced into the framework or on surface of CDs@UiO-66 to obtain the final material Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66. The morphologies of the precursor before and after modification were characterized by SEM. Fig. 1 a and b were the SEM images of UiO-66, and it could be observed that the material was composed of uniform nanospheres. Fig. 1c and d showed the SEM images of the composite material CDs@UiO-66 obtained after modification by CDs at different magnifications, and it was found that the uniform nanoparticles became rougher by comparison. Fig. 1e and f exhibited the SEM images of the final target material Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 at different magnifications. It can be observed that the morphology maintained basically unchanged and still showed uniform nanosphere morphology. In addition, the element mapping in Fig. 1g indicated that Zr, O, Ag, Eu and N elements were evenly distributed throughout the material, and further implied that CDs, Eu3+ and Ag + had been successfully introduced into UiO-66 and the target material was successfully prepared.

Fig. 2 a showed XRD patterns of UiO-66, CDs@UiO-66, Eu/CDs@UiO-66 and Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66. UiO-66 exhibited three featured peaks corresponding to the (111), (200) and (600) crystal plane diffractions, respectively [47]. The XRD patterns of the synthesized UiO-66, CDs@UiO-66, Eu/@CDsUiO-66 and Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 were all consistent with the simulated result of UiO-66, demonstrating successful synthesis of UiO-66. Furthermore, it was proven that the incorporation of CDs, Eu3+ and Ag + had no impact on the main framework structure of UiO-66. In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. S1), the broad absorption band at ∼3420 cm−1 was caused by the stretching vibrations of N–H and O–H bonds. The peak at ∼2931 cm−1 belonged to the typical stretching mode of C–H. Besides, the peak located at ∼1560 cm−1 was ascribed to the typical N–H bending vibration. The peak at ∼1420 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the C–N bond. All characteristic peaks of CDs existed in IR spectra of CDs@UiO-66, indicating that CD had successfully been encapsulated in UiO-66. By comparing the XPS spectra of UiO-66 and Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 in Fig. 2b, it was known that characteristic peaks of Eu3+ 3d and Ag+ 3d appeared in the composite, indicating successful functionalization of UiO-66 by Eu3+ and Ag+. Fig. 2c and d revealed that the surface area and average pore size of BET decreased significantly after gradual modification, indicating that the pores of UiO-66 were blocked by some CDs, Eu3+ and Ag+, which further proved that the three species have been successfully introduced into UiO-66 and the Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 composite has been successfully synthesized.

The luminescence properties of different materials dispersed in aqueous solution were explored under identical excitation condition, and the fluorescent spectra were shown in Fig. S2. It can be observed that the pure UiO-66 had a weak and broad emission band from 400 nm to 500 nm and CDs displayed a strong emission peak at 440 nm. It is worth noting that CDs@UiO-66 also showed a strong emission peak at 440 nm, indicating that the blue fluorescence came from CDs, which further confirmed the successful synthesis of CDs@UiO-66 composite. Interestingly, when Eu3+ was further introduced into CDs@UiO-66, the resulting composite Eu/CDs@UiO-66 emitted strong characteristic fluorescence of Eu3+ in the wavelength range of 570–700 nm besides CDs fluorescence at 440 nm. By a comparison it was found that fluorescence intensity of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 at 613 nm obviously reduced but the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm slightly increased. As expected, under the excitation wavelength of 290 nm, Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 showed 5 characteristic emission peaks of Eu3+ at 578, 590, 613, 650 and 696 nm. These peaks are attributed to the characteristic narrow-band emissions of Eu3+, which arise from the electron transitions between the excited state (5D0) and the ground states (7FJ: J = 0–4) of Eu3+, facilitated by the “antenna effect” from ligand to Eu3+ [35,51,52]. Besides, in order to study the stability of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 suspension under extreme conditions, the fluorescence intensity ratio I613/I440 of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 suspension was measured in different environments. It can be seen from Fig. S3, the I613/I440 of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 maintained stable in a wide pH range (3–10), which manifested that the Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 could be applied in acid-base environments. In addition, when Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 was dispersed in an aqueous solution and stored for approximately one week, its ratio fluorescence intensity (I613/I440) hardly changed (Fig. S4). This proved that the sensor had an excellent stability in a water environment and allowed subsequent practical application. Moreover, the effect of NaCl concentration on the relative fluorescence intensity (I613/I440) of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 suspension was also investigated. As showed in Fig. S5 I613/I440 exhibited enough stability at NaCl concentration ranging from 0.2 to 1.0 M, suggesting excellent stability of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 in high ionic strength. The above analyses demonstrated the exceptional luminescence stability of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 in various extreme environments, further affirming its huge potential for practical applications.

Cystinuria is a common chromosomal recessive disease. Cysteine is insoluble in urine and easy to reach saturation, which will lead to stone recurrence, urinary tract obstruction and urinary tract infection. In severe cases, it may lead to hydronephrosis and even kidney failure. Cystinuria mainly results from congenital defects of renal tubules and the resulting dysfunction of reabsorption of Arg. Therefore, it is crucial for early diagnosis and treatment, and prevention of cystinuria clinical symptoms to accurately detect Arg content in the human body. To demonstrate the feasibility of the fluorescent composite Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 for specific detection of Arg, the material was dispersed in aqueous solutions containing different amino acids and metal ions (Leu, Glu, Ala, Asp, Cys, Gly, His, Lys, Thr, Val, Met, Ser, Pro, Ser, Hom, Na+, K+, Ca2+), respectively, and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded and compared under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 3 a, Arg led to a significant change in the emission spectrum, whereas effects of other components were almost negligible. Fig. 3b showed that when Arg was added the characteristic emission intensity of Eu3+ at 613 nm significantly enhanced while the emission intensity CDs at 440 nm almost kept stable in Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 suspension (Fig. 3b) and the corresponding specific coordinates on the CIE chromaticity diagram moved from the blue area (0.2732, 0.2444) to the red area (0.3915, 0.2647) (Fig. 3c), which was in accord with the color change under the UV-lamp at 365 nm. These results indicated that the sensor can be used as a ratio fluorescence sensor and exhibited an outstanding selectivity for the detection of Arg. In addition, the anti-interference ability of the Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 sensor was also investigated by exploring effects of some potential interfering species such as Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, NaCl, KCl, Lle, Met, Lys, Val, Glu, Leu, Try) on Arg detection. As seen in Fig. 3d, the presence of other interfering substances had little effect on the ratio luminescence intensity of the composite suspension in presence of Arg, which further indicated that Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 was a highly selective probe for detecting Arg. Therefore, this sensing method can be used in the “turn-on” fluorescence detection of Arg in urine for early diagnosis and rapid screening of related diseases such as cystinuria.

To investigate the sensitivity of Arg detection, further experiments were carried out. The Arg solutions with increasing concentrations were gradually added to Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 suspension and their fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. The fluorescence intensity at 613 nm gradually increased with the increasing Arg concentration from 0 to 500 μM, while the fluorescence intensity at 440 nm remained unchanged in Fig. 4 a. A good linear correlation was observed between I613/I440 and Arg concentration in the range of 0–300 μM in Fig. 4b and the detection limit (LOD) was estimated to be 0.74 μM based on 3Sb/K, which was equivalent or even lower than the reported values in Table 1 . Consequently, this method is useful for determination of Arg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Arg in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: Arg is considered a very important biomarker of cystinuria and its quantitative determination in human body fluids is very important for early diagnosis of related diseases. As we know, pH value in human urine ranges 4.6–8.0 [50], in which the ratio fluorescence intensity I613/I440 of the sensing material was stable enough (Fig. S3), so it is possible to use the proposed sensor to quantitatively detect Arg in human urine sample. Considering the complexity of urine composition, we studied effect of possible urine components including different amino acids and inorganic salts on the fluorescent response of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 to arginine. It was found that the impact of these interference substances on the emission intensity (I613/I440) of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 was insignificant and minimal (Fig. S6). Consequently, this novel dual-emission Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 sensor with strong anti-interference ability can be effectively utilized for Arg detection in urine.

To evaluate the potential of the sensor Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 in practical application, it was employed to detect Arg in serum, urine and tea samples by standard addition method. The experimental results were presented in Table 2 . The recoveries were in the range of 96.65–104.2% and relative standard deviations (RSDs) were all below 3.04%. The results indicated that the prepared Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 could supply a well-pleasing platform for accurate detection of Arg in actual samples.

The possible fluorescent sensing mechanism was further investigated. Firstly, we observed the Arg caused morphology change. As showed in Fig. S7, SEM morphology of the Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 sample treated with aqueous Arg almost was in complete agreement with that of the untreated sample. It indicated that the morphology change of the probe material is not a cause to lead to the fluorescence enhancement. Subsequently, we measured XRD pattern of the sensing material after Arg treatment to justify whether any changes in the frame structure of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 resulted in an enhancement of the fluorescence intensity. It can be seen from Fig. S8 that the XRD patterns of the composite before and after Arg treatment almost remained unchanged. Therefore, the above results could exclude the possibility that the change in fluorescence intensity was caused by the change in morphology and framework collapse. Then we compared the fluorescence spectra of Eu/CDs@UiO-66 and Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 in the presence or absence of Arg. As shown in Fig. S9, the emission intensity of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 at 613 nm slightly decreased relative to Eu/CDs@UiO-66. But the presence of Arg led to a more significant enhancement in the emission intensity of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 at 613 nm than Eu/CDs@UiO-66. These results suggested that the significant fluorescent response of Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 to Arg may be attributed to the presence of Ag+ ion. We further compared the UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag+, Arg, and their mixed solution. From Fig. S10 it was evident that Arg induced a significant red shift in the characteristic absorption of Ag+ from 310 nm to 324 nm, which provided further evidence for the coordination between Ag+ ion and Arg. This coordination interaction led to a change in electron density of the ligand, which further sensitized emission of Eu3+ center. In addition, change in chemical composition of the composite largely affect the coordination environment of Eu3+ ion, thus changing its ground and excited states, which in turn also causes change in the fluorescence emission intensity [59,60]. According to crystal field theory, the crystal field can lead to the splitting of free lanthanide ion levels and electron cloud expansion [61]. Coordination of Ag+ and Arg may cause a change in the lattice environment of Eu3+, which further enhances the emission of hypersensitive 5D0→7F2 of Eu3+ center [35,62,63]. So it is speculated that the interaction between Arg and Ag+ could enhance the “antenna effect” between the ligand and Eu3+, thus causing the “turn-on” fluorescence emission at 613 nm [64].

In summary, this paper prepared a dual-emission fluorescence material Ag/Eu/CDs@UiO-66 based on CDs, Eu3+ and Ag + co-modification to UiO-66 by three-step post-synthesis modification method for the detection of Arg. Upon addition of Arg, the red fluorescence emission of Eu3+ was enhanced at 613 nm in the system, while the characteristic emission of CDs remained unchanged at 440 nm. Based on the particular luminescence property of the synthesized material, a sensitive ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed to realize the rapid detection of Arg in serum, urine and tea samples. This work provides a new selection for the early prevention, diagnosis and treatment of cystinuria.

Liping Peng: Writing - review & editing. Hao Guo: Funding acquisition. Ning Wu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Bingqing Liu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Mingyue Wang: Formal analysis, Software. Jiaying Tian: Project administration, Investigation, Resources. Borong Ren: Project administration, Investigation, Resources. Zhiguo Yu：Investigation, Resources. Wu Yang: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21665024), Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (20JR10RA083), Project of Gansu Excellent Doctoral (22JR5RA140), Northwest Normal University Young Teachers Research Capacity Promotion Plan (NWNU-LKQN-18-23), the Key Lab of Eco-Environments Related Polymer Materials of MOE, Key Lab of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province for their financial supports.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-performance liquid chromatographic assay for the quantitation of L-arginine in human plasma
